segments.s610-g1: به‌روز سدیگر is an adverbial temporal phrase 'on another day' (به‌روز + سِ دیگر); برون‌رفت here functions as the past action 'went out' (an older/poetic form) and شاه is the subject placed after the verb (inversion common in epic poetry).
segments.s610-g2: This is a nominal continuation/apposition giving who accompanied or was involved: ابا is a proper name here (not the verbal sense) and is followed by a coordination 'لشکر و ساز نخچیرگاه' = 'army and (the) hunting-music/instruments'; the phrase lacks an explicit verb and links to the previous clause as accompaniment.
segments.s610-g3: بزرگان ایران is a genitival phrase 'the great men of Iran'; ز بهر شکار is a purpose phrase 'for the sake of the hunt' where ز (here 'because/for') precedes بهر (literary 'for, toward'), together marking motive/purpose.
segments.s610-g4: به درگاه رفتند is the finite verb phrase 'went to the court' (رفتند = they went); سیصد سوار follows as a numeric specification 'three hundred horsemen' — here presented after the verb as an appositive/clarifying noun phrase telling how many or who went.
segments.s610-g5: ابا is a personal name (subject/agent here); هر سواری 'for each rider' is a distributive phrase (هر = 'each'); پرستنده سی means 'thirty followers/attendants' (پرستنده used as 'devotee/follower' and the numeral سی follows it in the original line).
segments.s610-g6: This is an enumerative prepositional list introduced by ز/از meaning 'from': ز ترک و ز رومی و از پارسی = 'from the Turks, from the Romans, and from the Persians.' The repeated preposition is a poetic emphatic listing of ethnic groups.
segments.s610-g7: پرستنده سیصد is a noun + numeral phrase 'three hundred attendants'; ز ایوان شاه is a prepositional/locative phrase 'from the king's iwan' (ز = from, and ایوان شاه = the king's hall/porch, genitive relation).
segments.s610-g8: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they departed/left'; با ساز نخچیرگاه is an instrumental/ accompanimental phrase 'with the hunting-ground's music/instruments' (با = with, ساز here = tune/music, نخچیرگاه qualifying it as 'of the hunting-ground').
segments.s610-g41: هرانکس که بودند نخچیرجوی is a relative clause: هرانکس 'whoever' + که introducing the clause; بودند is the past plural of 'to be' used here with a plural/collective sense ('were'); نخچیرجوی is a predicate noun 'hunter, seeker of game'. Word order is slightly inverted (relative + copula + predicate) as in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s610-g42: سوی آب دریا نهادند روی literally: 'toward the water of the sea they placed/set (their) face.' سوی = 'toward'; آب دریا = 'the sea's water' (the sea); نهادند is past plural 'they set/placed' (here used idiomatically with روی to mean 'turned (their) face'); روی = 'face'. The verb follows its object phrase, a common word order in classical Persian.
segments.s610-g43: جهاندار بهرام هر هفت سال is a nominal phrase: جهاندار 'the world‑ruler/sovereign' modifying the proper name بهرام; هر هفت سال is a temporal adverbial 'every seven years' placed after the name, as is typical in Persian.
segments.s610-g44: بدان آب رفتی به فرخنده فال should be read as 'بدان آب رفت (ی) به فرخنده فال' — 'he went to that water for a fortunate/auspicious omen.' بدَان here functions as an archaic fused preposition+demonstrative 'to that' (ب = 'to' + دان ~ 'that'); رفتی is a poetic/past form 'went' (3rd past); به introduces purpose 'for' and فرخنده فال = 'an auspicious omen.'
segments.s610-g45: چو لشکر به نزدیک دریا رسید is a temporal clause: چو = 'when'; لشکر = 'the army' (subject); به نزدیک دریا = 'to/near the sea' (adverbial phrase of place); رسید is the past 3rd singular 'reached/arrived.' The clause sets the scene for the following main clause.
segments.s610-g46: شهنشاه دریا پر از مرغ دید is the main clause with object phrase before the verb (typical poetic order): شهنشاه = 'the king/emperor' (subject); دریا پر از مرغ = 'the sea, full of birds' (differential noun + adjectival phrase using پر از 'full of'); دید = past 'saw.'
segments.s610-g47: بزد طبل و طغری شد اندر هوا: بزد طبل = 'he struck/beat the drum' (بزد past 3rd sing. + طبل object). و connects clauses. طغری شد اندر هوا = 'the tughra/tughra‑standard became/rose into the air' (طغری = royal emblem/standard; شد = 'became/was raised'; اندر هوا = 'in/into the air'). The order is poetic but straightforward.
segments.s610-g48: شکیبا نبد مرغ فرمانروا is a nominal sentence with negation: شکیبا = 'patient'; نبد is an archaic negative copular/verb meaning 'was not' or 'did not exist'; مرغ فرمانروا = 'the ruler's bird' (a hunting bird). Together: 'the sovereign's bird was not patient' (i.e., it grew impatient).
segments.s610-g33: This is a noun phrase giving what was sent: 'شتروار' is an adverbial/qualifying word meaning 'in long files/rows' placed before the numeral and noun; 'سیصد طرایف' is the numeral + plural noun 'three hundred curiosities/oddities'; 'ز چین' is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from China' with 'ز' = 'from' placed before the origin. Word order is qualifier + number + noun + origin (typical in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s610-g34: 'فرستاد' is the simple past verb 'he sent' (3rd person). The conjunction 'و' links the two items sent. 'یاقوت سیصد نگین' is an inverted noun phrase 'three hundred rubies' (literally 'ruby three-hundred gems') where 'یاقوت' (ruby) qualifies 'نگین' (gems).
segments.s610-g35: 'پس' is a temporal/conclusive adverb 'then'. 'بازداران' is a noun (agents) — here contextually 'falconers / keepers of hunting birds' (older usage), rendered as the plural agent. 'صد و شست یوز' is a numeral phrase 'one hundred and sixty cheetahs' (صد = 100, و = and, شست = 60) where the counted noun 'یوز' (cheetah/hunting animal) follows the numeral.
segments.s610-g36: 'ببردند' is the past plural verb 'they took/carried away'. The preposition 'با' marks accompaniment 'with' and introduces 'شاه گیتی فروز' — a compound epithet where 'شاه' (king) is qualified by 'گیتی فروز' (literally 'world‑illuminating' or 'world‑glorious'), i.e. 'the king who lights/illuminates the world'.
segments.s610-g37: 'بیاراسته' here functions as a participial/adjectival form meaning 'adorned / having been adorned' (an older poetic usage) modifying the following noun phrase. 'طوق یوز' is a genitive construction 'the cheetah's collar' and 'از گهر' is an of‑/made‑of phrase 'of gems' (i.e. a collar of jewels). The overall unit describes a collar of jewels that has been adorned.
segments.s610-g38: 'بدو' is the clitic/prepositional pronoun 'to it / upon it' (referring back to the collar). 'اندر افگنده' is a compound verbal phrase in past participle/past form meaning 'had placed / had cast upon' (اندر = 'on/in', افگنده = 'placed/cast'); 'زنجیر زر' is the object 'a chain of gold' (زنجیر = chain, زر = gold) which has been fastened onto the collar.
segments.s610-g39: 'بیامد' is the past tense 'he came'. 'شهنشاه' is the lofty title 'the emperor'. 'زین سان' is an archaic idiom meaning 'in this way / thus' (زین = 'this', سان = 'manner'), and 'به دشت' is the prepositional phrase 'into the plain'. Together: 'Thus the emperor came into the plain.'
segments.s610-g40: 'همی' is an emphatic/progressive particle often used in older Persian; here it gives an emphatic tone ('indeed' / 'as it were'). 'تاجش' is 'his crown' (tāj + possessive suffix). 'از مشتری' is an ablative/compare phrase 'beyond/from Jupiter' (مشتری = the planet Jupiter used as a comparative or astrological reference). 'برگذشت' is the past verb 'passed by/surpassed'. The phrase implies that his crown surpassed or outshone Jupiter.
segments.s610-g73: ز here is a short poetic preposition meaning 'because of' or 'at the sight of'; it introduces the cause. The clause follows Persian predicative order where the thing affected (چشم او) precedes the verbal predicate خیره شد — شد is the past-tense/resultative 'became' (here 'was rendered/stunned').
segments.s610-g74: The second ز is the same causal/ablative 'because of'. باز here is the noun 'hawk/falcon' (a common poetic image). طغری is 'tughra' or royal emblem/inscription; دلش ('his heart') is a possessive construction with enclitic ش after دل. تیره شد uses تیره 'dark/bleak' plus شد 'became' to mean 'became dark/sombre'. The coordinating و links two causes 'because of the hawk and because of the tughra'.
segments.s610-g75: چو here introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. دهقان پرمایه is a noun phrase 'the wealthy/ample-resourced peasant' (پرمایه = well-off/substantial). او را uses او as the direct object with the object marker را; بدید is the simple past 'saw'. The clause thus means 'when the prosperous peasant saw him'.
segments.s610-g76: رخ او شد = 'his face became' (رخ 'face' + possessive او + شد 'became'). از بیم is an ablative/cause phrase 'from/because of fear' (از...بیم). چون here means 'like/as' introducing the simile, and شنبلید is the noun 'fenugreek' (a plant) used metaphorically — the line compares his fearful face to fenugreek (a poetic simile).
segments.s610-g77: This is a nominal phrase listing persons: خردمند پیری 'the wise elder/Pir-i' and برزین 'Barzin' with the idiom به نام 'by name' (به + نام = 'named/called'). Here به is the particle used in the fixed phrase به نام meaning 'by the name of / called'. The و simply joins the two names or epithets.
segments.s610-g78: دل او شد = 'his heart became' (دل 'heart' + او possessive + شد 'became'). از شاه gives the cause 'because of the king' (از = 'because of' here), and ناشادکام is an adjective meaning 'unhappy/ill-fated' describing the resulting state. The whole clause: 'his heart became unhappy because of the king.'
segments.s610-g79: برفت is the past verb 'he departed/went away' (here the verb of motion). از بر حوض برزین is a locative phrase 'from beside Barzin's pool' (از 'from', بر 'by/near' used with حوض 'pool' and the name برزین). چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' — چو = 'as/like' and باد = 'wind', giving the manner of his departure.
segments.s610-g80: بر شاه شد here means 'he went/took himself toward the king' (بر + شاه expresses movement/direction toward the king), شد is the past/resultative 'came/arrived' and introduces the next action. خاک را بوسه داد is a standard idiom 'kissed the earth' (خاک 'earth' + را object marker; بوسه 'kiss' + داد 'gave') meaning he prostrated or showed deep respect.
segments.s610-g25: سیاهی is a nominal/adjectival description 'blackness' placed before two locative phrases; به + noun marks location/association ('on/in/at the ...') and is repeated for each coordinate (به چنگ و به منقار). و simply links the two phrases. زرد is a predicate/adjectival modifier attached to منقار (the second coordinated noun) rather than to چنگ.
segments.s610-g26: چو is the comparative/simile particle 'like/as', introducing a simile; زر is the standard noun 'gold'; درخشنده is an adjectival/participle 'shining' modifying زر; بر is the preposition 'on/upon' linking the simile to the ground of comparison; لاژورد is the object of بر ('lapis lazuli'). The whole phrase functions as a simile modifying the preceding description.
segments.s610-g27: همی is an adverbial/progressive particle meaning 'continually/kept on' here. خواندش is خواند + object-suffix -ش and means 'he called/named it'; شاه is the agent 'the king'; طغری is the name/title given (a tughra); به نام is the fixed prepositional phrase 'by the name (of)'. Word order is Subject (شاه) + Verb (خواندش) with the object/name following.
segments.s610-g28: دو چشمش is the noun phrase 'his two eyes' (دو = numeral + چشمش = 'his eyes'). به رنگ is the prepositional phrase 'in the color of'; پر here means 'feather' (used for a color simile). از introduces the material/content 'of/from blood' and links to دو جام, a metaphor 'two cups' — together forming a pictorial image: the two eyes were (in) feather-color and like two goblets full of blood. Word order places the metaphorical apposition after the head noun.
segments.s610-g29: که is the subordinating particle 'that/which' introducing a relative clause. خاقان is the agent 'the Khaghan'. چینش here functions as a genitive/attribute (intended 'of China' in the historical phrase), and فرستاده (participle/past form) with بود as auxiliary yields the past perfective sense 'had sent'. The clause as a whole means 'which the Khaghan of China had sent.'
segments.s610-g30: یکی is the indefinite numeral 'one / a certain' introducing the item. تخت is the head noun 'throne'. با 'with' links تخت to تاج 'crown'. بیجاده appears as a descriptive adjective after تاج (here glossed 'roadless' in the given sense list). بود is the past copula 'was', completing the predication 'there was one throne with a crown (bi-jadah)'.
segments.s610-g31: یکی again means 'one / a certain'. طوق is the head noun 'collar/necklace'. زرین is an adjectival form 'golden'. زبرجد names the gemstone 'peridot' and functions as an attributive (inlaid with/containing). نگار here appears as a qualifying word attached to the jewel description (an ornamental/embellishing term in context). The word order is determiner + head + successive modifiers.
segments.s610-g32: This is a list of the decorative elements: چهل is 'forty' modifying یاره (here a counted decorative unit), followed by و 'and' and سی و شش 'thirty-six' modifying گوشوار 'ear‑pendants / earrings'. The coordination with و enumerates the pieces adorning the collar.
segments.s610-g9: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/with' introducing the material; دیبا names the material (brocade). بیاراسته here functions as an adjectival/predicative form 'adorned' (an archaic, poetic usage) that modifies صد شتر ('a hundred camels'). صد is the numeral ('a hundred') preceding the noun شتر (camel) in this poetic word order.
segments.s610-g10: رکابش is a noun + 3sg possessive suffix ('his/its stirrup'); the clause is verbless (the copula 'was' is omitted) so همه زر predicates the stirrups ('were all gold'). پالانش is 'his saddle‑cloth' and در is a locative/adverbial 'in/with/therein', referring back to the brocade just mentioned (i.e. the saddle‑cloth was of that brocade).
segments.s610-g11: ده + اشتر is a numeral phrase 'ten camels' (numeral before noun, poetic order). نشستنگه شاه را is a definite nominal phrase 'the king's seat' where را marks the definite/accusative/topic — the whole segment is nominal and implies an omitted verb ('made/served as').
segments.s610-g12: به is a preposition 'for/with' introducing material; دیبا names the material (brocade). بیاراسته (archaic/adjectival) modifies گاه 'place' — گاه here functions as 'place/seat' (poetic usage rather than the common sense 'time'). را again is the definite/object marker. The segment specifies that the king's seat/place was adorned with brocade.
segments.s610-g13: به پیش اندر is a spatial prepositional cluster 'in front, within/at the fore'; آراسته is an adjectival/predicative 'arrayed/adorned' that attaches to هفت پیل ('seven elephants') — هفت (seven) precedes پیل (elephant) in this poetic order. The clause is descriptive and lacks an overt copula.
segments.s610-g14: برو here is an archaic/poetic prepositional or connective element (contracted from بر 'on/upon' in classical usages) linking to تخت 'throne/seat'. تخت پیروزه is 'a turquoise (piroozeh) throne' (پیروزه = turquoise/color/stone) and همرنگ نیل is a simile 'of the same color as indigo' (همرنگ = same color + نیل = indigo). Together they describe the throne placed upon/with the elephants.
segments.s610-g15: همه quantifies 'all' and modifies پایه تخت ('the bases/pedestals of the throne' — پایه + تخت). زر و بلور list the materials 'gold and crystal'; again the clause is verbless, so 'were' is implied ('all the throne's supports were gold and crystal').
segments.s610-g16: نشستنگه شاه بهرام گور is a nominal phrase 'the seat/place of the king Bahram Gur.' شاه ('king') followed by the proper name بهرام گور (Bahrām Gūr, where گور is the epithet 'Gur' = onager) gives the full title; the segment names whose seat is described above.
segments.s610-g65: زمینش is زمین + possessive -ش = “his land/country.” به here introduces the material/instrument (how it was dressed) and links to دیبا. دیبا = “brocade.” بیاراسته is a poetic past‑participle functioning predicatively (“(was) adorned/dressed”); note the provided sense list did not include this positive meaning, so the best interpretation in context is an adjectival/predicative past form meaning “adorned.”
segments.s610-g66: همه باغ = “the whole/entire garden” (همه before the noun). پر follows the noun and means “full (of).” بنده و خواسته are a coordinated phrase: بنده = “servants/slaves,” and خواسته here functions as an adjective or substantivized adjective “the desired/desired ones.” The phrase means the garden is filled with servants and those desired/desired things/people.
segments.s610-g67: سه دختر = “three maidens” (numeral before noun). بر او نشسته is a locative expression meaning “sat by/near him” (بر + pronoun او). چو عاج is a simile modifying their appearance — “like ivory” — placed after the verb as a descriptive tag.
segments.s610-g68: نهاده is the perfect/past participle “placed/put” indicating the action the maidens have done. به + سربر = “on the head” (به marks the target/location). ز introduces the material/source of the crown: ز پیروزه تاج = “a crown of turquoise.” Word order is material introduced by ز before تاج.
segments.s610-g69: به رخ = “in/at (regard to) the face” where به marks the domain of the attribute; چون بهار sets up a simile: “(her) face like spring.” The clause is paralleled by و به بالا بلند, where به بالا = “in the upper (part)” or “above” and بلند = “tall/lofty,” giving a parallel descriptive line.
segments.s610-g70: Each small phrase is a metaphor introduced by به: به ابرو کمان = “(her) brow (is) an arc/bow” (کمان as curved shape), and به گیسو کمند = “(her) hair (is) a snare/lasso” (کمند used metaphorically for hair that ensnares). The structure is noun + metaphor (noun) with و linking the two images.
segments.s610-g71: یکی جام = “one cup” (یکی = a certain/one). بر دست = “on the hand” (بر = on/upon). هر یک = “each one” — the phrase means each of them had a cup. بلور after this acts as an appositive/description: “a crystal (cup).” The construction is verbless description of possession/holding.
segments.s610-g72: بدیشان (به + ایشان contracted) = “to them/for them.” نگه is the noun “gaze/look” and together نگه کرد (کرد + noun) is a common periphrasis meaning “directed a look” or “looked.” The subject appears at the end (بهرام گور): verb phrase + subject order is a poetic inversion: “Bahram Gur looked at them.”
segments.s610-g177: به دو (preposition + numeral/pronoun) = “to the two” (dative); گفت is the past tense verb ‘said’ placed after the addressee in poetic inversion; برزین is the subject (proper name) appearing after the verb by inversion. که‌ای شهریار is a quoted/vocative phrase: که here introduces the quoted words and ای (attached as که‌ای) marks the vocative “O,” followed by شهریار “king.”
segments.s610-g178: بتو = 'to you' (dative). شاد بادا is an optative wish: adjective شاد 'happy' with the optative particle بادا 'may it be', so literally 'may you be happy.' می‌و میگسار are coordinated nouns 'wine/fruit and cupbearer' giving the wished provision; placement after بادا expresses the content of the wish (’may there be wine and a cupbearer for you’).
segments.s610-g179: که here functions as the complementizer introducing reported speech ('that'/'that he said'); یارست is a predicative adjective/noun 'is beloved; is a lover' (copula implied). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (placed in the line as poetic word order). این خود اندر جهان is a noun phrase with emphatic خود 'very/itself' modifying این 'this' and اندر جهان 'in the world' as a locative adverbial.
segments.s610-g180: که again introduces a relative/complement clause ('who/that'); دارد is the present/continuous 'has/possesses' (here stative possession); چنین 'such' modifies زهره, which here names the poetic concept Venus/beauty (i.e. an ideal/fortunate woman); اندر نهان 'in secret' (adverbial phrase) follows the verb and indicates manner/location of possession.
segments.s610-g181: مرا‌گر is an archaic fused conditional structure (مرا + گر) meaning 'if (you accept) me' or literally 'if to me'; پذیری is 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you accept' (conditional sense). بسان 'like/as' and رهی 'a wayfarer' form the comparative phrase 'as a wayfarer.'
segments.s610-g182: که introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that/in order that' (here giving the intended result). بپرستم is 1st-person subjunctive/present with the old verbal prefix بـ meaning 'may I worship' or 'I will worship.' این تخت شاهنشهی is the direct object 'this imperial throne' (شاهنشهی modifies تخت). The clause expresses the conditional result: 'so that I may worship this imperial throne.'
segments.s610-g183: پرستش کنم pairs the noun 'worship' with the verb کنَم (1st-person subjunctive/optative) meaning 'I may do' / 'I will do' — here idiomatically 'I will worship.' تاج و تخت ترا lists 'crown and throne' with the possessive clitic ترا (here written as ت + را) attached to the final noun and referring back to both nouns: 'your crown and throne.'
segments.s610-g184: همان is an emphatic demonstrative 'that very' / 'the same'; فر و اورنگ و بخت lists three related royal attributes (فر 'royal glory', اورنگ 'throne/glory', بخت 'fortune'). ترا again is the possessive clitic 'of you' attached to the final noun and applying to the list. The phrase reinforces and specifies what the speaker vows to revere.
segments.s610-g129: برفتند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural 'they went/advanced.' هر سه follows the verb to specify 'the three (of them).' به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase indicating direction/destination ('to/near the king'); word order (verb + subject phrase + prepositional phrase) is natural in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s610-g130: نهادند is past 3rd‑person plural of نهادن 'they placed/put.' بر سر is the locative expression 'upon the head' and ز گوهر کلاه is a genitive/material phrase 'a cap of jewel(s)' using poetic ز = 'of/from' to mark material; the clause means 'they placed a jeweled cap upon (his) head.'
segments.s610-g131: This is a nominal/telegraphic description: یکی 'one' + پای کوب ('foot‑beater' = one who stamps his foot) uses a noun+verb compound where کوب is the root meaning 'to beat/strike.' و دگر چنگ‌زن 'and another [is a] harp‑player'—there is no main verb because this is a list of performers/roles.
segments.s610-g132: سه دیگر 'the three others' is followed by two attributive compounds: خوش‑آواز ('sweet‑voiced, melodious') describes their singing, and لشکر‑شکن is a compound epithet 'army‑breaker' (hyperbolic epithet). Again there's no verb—this is a descriptive phrase attached to the three others.
segments.s610-g133: به آواز ایشان is an instrumental/causal prepositional phrase 'by/at their song.' شهنشاه is the subject 'the sovereign/emperor,' and جام is the (near) object 'cup.' The clause sets up the action that follows: 'at their song the king (the) cup ...'.
segments.s610-g134: ز باده تهی کرد is a verb phrase where ز باده 'of wine' marks what the cup contained and تهی کردن (تهی + کردن) means 'to make empty / to empty.' کرد is the light verb 'made/did' (causative sense). و شد شادکام 'and became joyful' uses شد (past of شدن) plus the predicate adjective شادکام 'glad/fortunate.'
segments.s610-g135: بدو is the clitic/prepositional phrase 'to him' (indirect object). گفت is the past 'he said' (introducing reported speech). کاین دختران کیند is an embedded interrogative/relative 'who are these maidens'—کیند is an archaic 3rd‑person plural copula/question form ('are they/who are they').
segments.s610-g136: که introduces the relative/subordinate clause 'who ...' با تو 'with you' marks accompaniment, بدین is the compressed form of به این 'in/with this' (classical spelling b + دین here) meaning 'in such/this,' شادمانی is the noun 'joy,' and زیند is the archaic/present 3rd‑person plural 'they live.' The whole clause = 'who live with you in this joy.'
segments.s610-g233: بیامد is the simple past (3rd person) 'came' used as the main verb. به is the preposition 'to/toward/into' introducing the following noun phrase. مشکوی زرین خویش is a stacked adjectival phrase: مشکوی 'musk‑scented' and زرین 'golden' qualify an implied dwelling/garment; خوی + ش is a possessive construction where ش is the enclitic 'his/her/its' and خوی (here functioning as the base of the possessive) yields 'his own'. Poetic word order allows the adjectives before/around the possessive suffix.
segments.s610-g234: سوی is a preposition 'toward(s)/to' marking direction. خانه is the head noun 'house/room'. عنبر آگین is a compound adjectival phrase modifying خانه: عنبر ('amber') + آگین (an adjectival element meaning 'adorned/bright/imbued with') — together 'amber‑adorned' or 'perfumed with amber'. خوی + ش again forms the possessive 'his own'. The phrase as a whole means 'toward his amber‑adorned house'.
segments.s610-g235: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when...came' (چو = 'when', آمد = past 'came'). یکی هفته is the numeral phrase 'one week' placed after the verb in this poetic inversion. آنجا 'there' gives the location. ببود is an archaic past form of 'بود' meaning 'was/there remained' — together the clause means 'when one week came, he remained there'.
segments.s610-g236: بسی is an adverb 'much'/'a great deal'. خورد is the past 'ate' (third person). و links clauses 'and'. بخشید here means 'gave/bestowed' (not 'forgave'). و شادی نمود is a light‑verb construction: شادی 'celebration/joy' + نمود (past of نمودن, 'made/shown') → 'made merry' or 'displayed rejoicing'. The sequence lists his actions during the stay.
segments.s610-g97: "هم‌انگه" is a temporal adverb meaning "at that moment." "یکی" is indefinite here, "a certain." "بنده را" is the accusative/object-marked phrase "the servant" (را = direct-object marker), and word order is inverted: object + verb + subject, so "گفت شاه" means "the king said (to) the servant."
segments.s610-g98: "که" here introduces reported speech ("that/that he said"). "رو" is the noun "face" (used in the idiom "رو کردن/رو کن" = "turn (one's) face"). "گوزبن" is a proper name (Gūzban) rather than the animal sense. "کن" is the imperative of "کردن" ("do/turn!"), giving the command "turn your face (toward) Gūzban." "سراسر نگاه" is an adverbial phrase "look all over" or "gaze throughout."
segments.s610-g99: "بشد" is a narrative past/become (here "became" / "he was"), not an optative. "بنده" is the subject "the servant." "چون باد" is a simile "like the wind," implying swift movement. "و آواز داد" is a light-verb construction where "آواز داد" = "uttered a cry/raised his voice" ("داد" = gave).
segments.s610-g100: "که" introduces the content of the cry (reported wish). "همواره" = "always/ever." "شاه جهان" = "king of the world" (noun + genitive-like modifier). "باد" is the classical optative particle here ("may he be"). "شاد" is the predicative adjective "happy/merry." Together they form the wish: "May the king of the world be ever happy."
segments.s610-g101: "که" here introduces explanatory content ('for/that'). "طغری" is the royal monogram/tughra. "به شاخی" means "on/onto a branch" (شاخی = a branch or branch-like). "برآویختست" is a perfective/past stative meaning "has been hung/raised" (lit. "has been hung up on").
segments.s610-g102: "کنون" = "now." "بازدارش" is an imperative form meaning "stop/prevent it" (بازدار + clitic -ش = "it"). "بگیرد" is a jussive/optative 3rd-person singular ("let him seize/take"). "به" + "دست" = "into the hand / by the hand" (i.e., "seize it in his hand"). The line issues the command to prevent/hold and have it taken into hand.
segments.s610-g103: "چو" = "when." "طغری پدید آمد" is a verbal phrase "the tughra appeared/was revealed" (پدید آمد = appeared). "آن پیر گفت" = "that old man said" (آن = that; پیر = old man; گفت = said). This is a straightforward temporal clause followed by the speech-reporting clause.
segments.s610-g104: "که‌ای" is the combination of the reporting/complementizer "که" plus the vocative particle "ای" (so: 'that he said, "O..."'). "بر زمین" = "on the earth / upon the land" (modifier of the king). "شاه" = "king." "بی‌بار" = "without offspring/childless/barren." "و جفت" = "and without a mate/partner" (جفت here = mate/spouse). Together this is the elder's direct address: "O king of the earth, childless and without a mate."
segments.s610-g105: بدین فقره reads as an optative clause: باد at the end is the optative particle meaning “may (it be)”. فرخنده is a predicative adjective modifying the (implied) subject مرزبان; بر تو is a prepositional phrase ‘upon you/to you’ placed before the predicative. Word order here is Verb-like Optative (باد) at the end, with the adjective and its prepositional complement preceding it.
segments.s610-g106: This is a parallel optative clause: باد again expresses a wish. همه تاجداران is the plural subject ‘all the crowned ones/rulers’; ترا is the object ‘to you’ expressed as the enclitic ت plus the object marker را (archaic/poetic ordering). بنده is a predicate noun ‘servant’ that with باد yields ‘may they be servants to you.’
segments.s610-g107: بدین شادی is an adverbial phrase ‘on/for this joy’ (بدین = bi + this, treated here as a fixed prepositional/ adverbial unit). اکنون ‘now’ reinforces timing. یکی جام is the direct object ‘one cup’ placed before the verb; خواه is the present/imperative form of خواستن used here as an exhortation ‘ask for/seek’ (second person implied).
segments.s610-g108: چو marks a temporal subordinate clause ‘when’. آرام دل ‘the heart at rest’ is a nominal phrase (adjective + noun). یافتی is the 2nd-person past perfective ‘you found/obtained’, and the following main clause کام خواه uses خواه again as an imperative/optative ‘seek/ask for your desire’. So the structure is: subordinate temporal clause (چو … یافتی) followed by the exhortation.
segments.s610-g109: This is a locative noun phrase: شهنشاه گیتی = ‘the king/emperor of the world’ (title + genitive modifier). بران is an archaic/deictic prepositional element ‘upon/onto that’ followed by آبگیر ‘pond/reservoir’ giving the place where the action occurs. The whole phrase supplies the subject and location for the verb to follow.
segments.s610-g110: فرود آمد is a periphrastic verbal expression: فرود (alighting/descent) + آمد (came) = ‘alighted/ descended’. و coordinates to the next clause. شادمان گشت پیر is structured as predicate adjective (شادمان) + copular verb گشت ‘became’ + noun پیر ‘the old man/elder’ (the one who became joyful). Word order places the adjective before the copula and the referent.
segments.s610-g111: بیامد is a past tense 3rd person ‘came/arrived’. هم‌انگاه is an adverb ‘immediately/at the same moment’. دستور اوی is a noun + possessive enclitic (اوی = his) meaning ‘his command’. The verb precedes the noun phrase here (a poetic ordering): ‘his command came immediately.’
segments.s610-g112: همان is a demonstrative ‘that same/those same’. گنج داران is a compound plural ‘treasure-holders/treasurers’ (گنج + داران). The conjunction و links to گنجور اوی, where گنجور means ‘treasury/treasure-house’ and اوی is the possessive ‘his’, so ‘those treasure-keepers and his treasury’. This nominal string continues the object/theme of the previous verb (they are what came).
segments.s610-g89: دلم شد is a two-part expression: دل + ـم (my) gives “my heart,” and شد is the past of شدن meaning “became” (so ‘my heart became’ or ‘my heart tightened’). ازان is a prepositional/causal particle ‘from that / because of that,’ and مرغ گیرنده is a noun phrase where گیرنده (the active participle/type-former here) qualifies مرغ as ‘the capturing / capture-giving bird’ (i.e. a bird that seizes). تنگ is an adjective ‘narrow / constricted’ describing the state the heart took (شد + adjective = “became …”).
segments.s610-g90: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause (‘for / because’). مرغان is the plural subject ‘birds.’ چو here means ‘when’ (a temporal/conditional marker). نخچیر is ‘prey’ and indicates the condition under which the following predication holds. The phrase بد او پلنگ is elliptical: بد is an adjective ‘fierce / bad’ used predicatively, او = ‘he/it,’ and پلنگ is the predicate noun ‘leopard’; Persian often omits a copula, so the sequence means roughly ‘he is a leopard (to them)’ — i.e. ‘he behaves like a leopard toward them.’
segments.s610-g91: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the following noun; پاسخ 'answer' is the object/thing given; آورد is the past tense of آوردن ‘to bring’ used here idiomatically as ‘gave/uttered’ (i.e. 'returned/gave an answer'); برزین is the agent/name, and به شاه marks the indirect object ‘to the king.’ The word order is emphatic (چنین پاسـخ آورد برزین به شاه = 'thus an answer Barzin brought to the king').
segments.s610-g92: که introduces reported speech or content clause (‘that’). اکنون = 'now / just now.' یکی here means ‘a certain / one’ (indefinite). مرغ = 'bird.' دیدم is the past, first-person singular ‘I saw’ (object precedes the verb, a normal Persian word order variant). سیاه after the verb is a predicate/adjective ‘black’ describing the bird just seen.
segments.s610-g93: ابا here is an archaic/poetic form equivalent to با ‘with’ (common in epic diction) introducing an instrumental phrase. زنگ زرین is a compound meaning ‘a golden gleam/tinge’ or ‘golden ringing/shine’ modifying تنش ‘his body’ (تن + ـش = 'his body'). همچو قیر is a simile ‘like pitch/tar’ describing the overall appearance (so: 'with a golden sheen, his body was like tar'). Note the adjective-like simile follows the noun it qualifies, a frequent poetic order.
segments.s610-g94: همان here means ‘that very / the same’ (emphatic). چنگ in this context is 'claw/talon' (not harp), و is the coordinating 'and,' منقار او = 'his beak' (منقار + او). چون introduces a comparison 'like/as,' and زریر is a proper name used as the point of comparison (i.e. his talons and beak resembled Zarīr's). As before, the copula is omitted; the phrase reads as a direct simile.
segments.s610-g95: بیامد is the simple past ‘he came.’ بران = بر + آن, ‘upon that / on it’ (indicating where he alighted). گوزبن is a noun here referring to the hunter/falconer/agent (an occupational or dialectal name; context shows a person who would alight on the bird), and بر نشست is the verb phrase ‘sat upon’ or ‘alighted on’ (نشست used as past/preterite 'sat'). The clause narrates the arrival and alighting of that hunter/agent.
segments.s610-g96: بیاید is a jussive/imprecative form here meaning 'may he come / may it come' (optative/wish). هم‌اکنون 'right now.' به بختت is a dative phrase 'to/into your bāxt' — bāxt meaning fate/fortune; the possessive ـت marks 'your.' به دست 'into the hand' is literal and idiomatic: together به بختت به دست = 'into your fortune — into your hand,' i.e. 'may he fall into your lot / into your hands.'
segments.s610-g193: بگویم is a 1st-person present/subjunctive/formal-polite verb-form used performatively here as 'I will tell' or 'let me tell'. کنون is an adverb 'now' placed after the verb for emphasis. هرچ ... نهان is a compact clause meaning 'whatever exists hidden' (هرچ = 'whatever'; هستم is used here as an existential/copular element 'is/exists' in an older/poetic usage; نهان = 'hidden'). Word order is verb + temporal adverb + indefinite + existential + adjective, a typical classical Persian sequence.
segments.s610-g194: بد و نیک is a balanced nominal pair 'the bad and the good' (adjectives used as nouns). با شهریار جهان is a prepositional phrase introduced by با, here functioning in the sense 'in the presence of/with/regarding the' the sovereign — classical Persian allows با to indicate address or company. شهریار جهان is a genitival phrase 'the sovereign (lord) of the world.'
segments.s610-g195: ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' introducing an item in a list. پوشیدنی is a deverbal/nominalized form (the -نی suffix) meaning 'that which is worn' or 'garments'; هم 'also/likewise' connects the items; the repeated ز before گستردنی continues the same listing. گستردنی is another -نی nominal meaning 'things to be spread/laid out.' The construction is a serial list of categories introduced by ز.
segments.s610-g196: Again ز opens the next item. افگندنی is the -نی deverbal nominal 'things to be cast/thrown' or 'that which is to be placed/scattered.' و is the conjunction 'and'. پراگندگی is a noun meaning 'dispersion' or 'scattering' and completes the paired listing. The whole segment continues the enumerative catalogue of kinds of possessions.
segments.s610-g197: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/truly' placed before the statement. شتربار is a compound noun 'camel-load(s)/caravan cargo' that names the quantity's unit. باشد here is a modal/copular form used in classical Persian to state existence/quantity ('(there) is/may be'); its exact nuance is archaic/optative rather than the supplied dictionary senses.
segments.s610-g198: به ایوان من is a locative/prepositional phrase 'at/to my iwan/porch' (به + place); ایوان من shows possessive order 'my iwan'. بنده‌گر is a nominal predicate (here 'a servant/attendant' or 'one who tends servants' in older usage) and بیش نیست is an idiom meaning 'is no more than / there is nothing more than' (بیش = 'more' and نیست = 'is not'), i.e. 'only.'
segments.s610-g199: همان is a demonstrative/determiner 'that very' or 'the same'. It scopes over the following list: یاره and طوق (both items of ornamentation) joined by و 'and'. the second هم before تاج emphasizes 'also/likewise'. تاج و تخت 'crown and throne' is another fixed pair (sovereign regalia). The segment is a noun list introduced by همان with coordinating conjunctions.
segments.s610-g200: کزان is a relative/prepositional compound 'from which' or 'by which' linking the list to the maidens' good fortune. دختران را is the marked object/topic 'the maidens' (را is the definite object/emphasis marker). بود is the past copula 'was/were' (here past-tense linking verb). نیک‌بخت is an adjective/noun 'fortunate' describing the maidens. Overall this is a relative clause: 'by which the maidens were fortunate.'
segments.s610-g153: This is a prepositional/comparative phrase introduced by the preposition به (to/at). دیدار (sight/meeting) is the head of the first noun phrase, ماهی is used poetically for the beautiful one / moon-like beloved, and بالای ساج is a coordinated second location phrase ('on top of the saj'). The two phrases are joined by و and together function as the circumstance/scene for the following verb.
segments.s610-g154: بنازد is a third-person singular classical/poetic present (from نازیدن) meaning here 'takes pride / shows off'; the verb comes before its subject in this poetic word order. بتو is an indirect-object phrase 'to/for you' (with the enclitic sense), and تخت شاهی و تاج is the compound subject (throne and crown, with شاهی qualifying تخت). The verb expresses that these objects 'show pride' toward you.
segments.s610-g155: خنک (pleasant/cool) is used as a predicate in front of the relative clause introduced by آنک (traditional relative 'the one who'). شبگیر is a compound noun 'night‑visitor' (one who comes at night). بیندت is the verb 'he sees you' (بیند + enclitic object ت), and روی is the object 'face'. The clause literally reads 'pleasant is the one who, a night‑visitor, sees your face.'
segments.s610-g156: This clause repeats the predicate structure: خنک آنک ('pleasant is he who') + یابد (he finds/obtains, third person present) followed by a noun phrase ز موی تو بوی ('a scent from your hair') where ز marks source. The verb is transitive and takes the nominal phrase 'بوی' as its object; تو is the possessor of موی.
segments.s610-g157: This is a descriptive noun phrase for bodily strength: میان تنگ ('with/in a narrow/muscular chest') followed by a simile چون شیر ('like a lion') and the coordinated phrase و بازو ستبر ('and a stout/strong arm'). Word order places adjectives after the nouns in classical style; the whole phrase describes the physical disposition of the person whose crown is spoken of next.
segments.s610-g158: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'always' or 'ever'. فر (royal splendour/glory) is the subject, تاجت is a genitive construction 'your crown' (تاج + enclitic -ت for 'your'), and برآید is the verb 'rises/appears'. به ابر ('to the cloud(s)') marks the upward extent. The clause means 'the splendour of your crown ever rises to the clouds.'
segments.s610-g159: به گلنار is a comparative phrase 'like the pomegranate blossom' (به + noun). ماند (from ماندن) here functions in a quasi‑copular/comparative way 'remains/looks like' and همی is an adverb 'ever/always.' چهر تو ('your face') is the topic/subject. The clause thus reads 'your face is ever like the pomegranate blossom.'
segments.s610-g160: This clause has the verb بخندد (third person present 'laughs') preceding its subject دل ('the heart') in poetic word order; به شادی ('with joy') is an adverbial phrase. از مهر تو ('because of / from your love') gives the cause. Altogether: 'the heart laughs with joy because of your love.'
segments.s610-g113: بیاورد is a past 3rd‑person form (classical/poetic variant of آورد) meaning “he brought.” In older Persian the finite verb can appear before its subject, so بیاورد + برزین yields “Barzin brought.” می‌سرخ و جام is the compound object: می‌سرخ (here a poetic word for the red wine/‘the red draught’) joined by و to جام (“cup”). The whole segment therefore reads V S O (verb + name + objects).
segments.s610-g114: نخستین is an adjective/noun “the first,” here referring back to the preceding جام (“the first cup”). ز is the poetic preposition “from/of” (often rendered as “that of / belonging to”). شاه جهان is a genitive phrase “king of the world.” برد is the past of بردن used here in the sense “to bear / to carry / to bear the name” (i.e. ‘that bore the name’); نام is “name” or “title.” Grammatically this is a relative/attributive clause qualifying the cup: “the first (cup) that bore the name of the king of the world.”
segments.s610-g115: بیاورد again is the past 3rd‑person “he brought.” خوان is a collective noun here meaning “table/banquet/feast,” and خورش is “stew / cooked dishes.” The coordinating و links the two objects. ساختند is past 3rd‑person plural “they made / arranged / set (out),” so the clause means “He brought the table and dishes; they arranged them.”
segments.s610-g116: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when” or “after.” از + خوردن + نان forms a gerund phrase “from/after the eating of bread” (i.e. when they had eaten the bread). بپرداختند is a past plural verb meaning roughly “they attended to / dealt with / finished (with)” — in this context it marks the completion of the bread course and the transition to the next actions (they then attended to the rest).
segments.s610-g117: ازان پس is a temporal sequence “after that / thereupon.” بیاورد is the past “he brought.” جامی is an indefinite or partitive form of جام (“a cup / a goblet”); the suffix -ی can indicate “a certain.” بلور is the adjective/noun “crystal,” placed after جامی to describe the cup (“a crystal cup”). The whole segment: “After that he brought a crystal cup.”
segments.s610-g118: نهادند is the past 3rd‑person plural of نهادن “to place / to set,” so نهادند بر دست = “they placed on the hand.” بر indicates position “on/upon,” دست is “hand,” and بهرام گور is the full name/title of the recipient (Bahrām + the epithet گور, here the onager epithet). The clause thus means “they placed it on Bahram Gūr’s hand.”
segments.s610-g119: جهاندار is a title “world‑holder / sovereign,” here qualifying بهرام (the name) in an appositional style: “the sovereign Bahram.” بستد is a past form meaning “he closed/ceased / put an end to” and here combines with نبید (an old word for wine) to mean “he stopped / ended the wine (drinking).” In classical Persian such short verb forms and noun order (title + name + verb + object) are common.
segments.s610-g120: از اندازه is a prepositional phrase meaning “beyond the measure / past the proper limit.” خط here means “line / boundary / limit” (i.e. a drawn boundary or limit), برتر is “higher / above / superior,” and کشید is the past of کشیدن “to draw.” The clause literally reads “he drew a line above the measure,” i.e. he marked/determined a limit beyond the usual measure. Note: classical poetic word order places the measure phrase before the verb.
segments.s610-g17: This clause expresses a distributive quantity: هر یکی ('for each one') + تیغ‌زن ('swordsman') is the topic, followed by the numeral phrase صد غلام ('a hundred slaves') as the (elliptical) predicate; the copula 'was/stood' is omitted and understood from context. Word order is typical Persian: determiner/quantifier (هر یکی) before the head noun (تیغ‌زن), then the numerical predicate (صد غلام).
segments.s610-g18: به introduces attendant/attire information ('with' / 'in'), followed by two coordinated noun phrases describing what the slaves wore: زرین کمرها ('golden girdles' – کمر here = girdle/belt, plural) و زرین ستام ('and golden ...'); زرین is an adjective placed before the noun as usual. The verb remains omitted and is supplied from the prior clause.
segments.s610-g19: This short clause gives a gift or provision: صد اشتر ('a hundred ashter/camels'—the exact lexical reading of اشتر is uncertain here) followed by بد, an adjective that qualifies the preceding noun. از بهر رامشگران is a prepositional phrase 'for the attendants/comforters' (بهر = 'for', رامشگران = 'those who give comfort/attendants'). The copula is again omitted and understood.
segments.s610-g20: همه ('all') is the subject/quantifier; بر سران افسر is a locative-possessive phrase 'on (their) heads, helmets' where بر = 'on/upon', سران = 'heads' (plural), and افسر = 'helmet' (here the helmet worn on the head). از گوهران ('of gems') qualifies the helmets. The verbal copula is again omitted (understood 'were' / 'they had').
segments.s610-g21: This clause begins like the others with a prepositional/associative marker (here rendered by the initial particle, uncertain in sense) plus بازداران ('holders of باز' — i.e., falconers contextually, though the listed lemma senses don't record that occupational meaning). The numerals صد و شست mean 'one hundred and sixty' (compound numeral), and باز here is the noun 'hawk/falcon'. The copula is again omitted.
segments.s610-g22: دو صد ('two hundred') modifies چرغ ('lamps/torches') followed by و + شاهین ('and [the] falcon'); گردن‌فراز is a postpositive adjective 'proud-necked' or 'proud' modifying شاهین. Numeral + noun order is standard (numeral before noun), and the clause lists items possessed/attended with the falconers.
segments.s610-g23: پس‌اندر is a temporal/discourse adverb 'afterwards/then'; یکی مرغ ('one bird') is the noun phrase (numeric determiner before noun). بودی is the past-tense copula 'was' (second/third-person archaic/poetic form), and سیاه is the adjective 'black' placed after the copula here for emphasis. Word order shows a topical adverb, then the existential/locative clause.
segments.s610-g24: گرامی‌تر is the comparative form 'more dear/more honored'; آن ('that') is the demonstrative subject; بود is the simple past copula 'was'. بر چشم شاه is an idiomatic locative-expression 'in the eye of the king' = 'to the king' (بر here indicates the perspective 'to/in the sight of'), completing the comparative clause 'that one was dearer to the king'.
segments.s610-g145: This clause is an introductory reporting phrase: 'بدان' = 'to that' (here marking the indirect object: 'to that minstrel'), followed by the person addressed 'چامه‌زن' (minstrel) and the verb 'گفت' (said). After the reporting verb comes the vocative 'کای ماه‌روی' — 'کای' is the archaic/vocative 'O' and 'ماه‌روی' is an epithet 'moon-faced/beautiful-faced' directly addressing the addressee.
segments.s610-g146: This is the reported imperative/command. 'بپرداز' is an imperative (ب- prefix + پرداز) meaning 'bestow / devote / give', with direct object 'دل' (heart). 'چامه شاه' is a possessive phrase without the ezāfe (typical in poetry) meaning 'the king's poem', and 'گوی' here functions as an imperative 'sing/recite' (poetic/deliberately archaic word-order: object + verb).
segments.s610-g147: Standard classical Persian SOV order: subject 'بتان' (literally 'idols' but here poetic 'the beauties') + objects 'چامه و چنگ' (poem and harp) + verb 'برساختند' (they raised/composed/they set to play). In epic/poetic usage 'بتان' often means beloved/beautiful women rather than literal idols.
segments.s610-g148: 'یکایک' is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn'; 'دل' is the (metaphorical) object 'hearts'; 'از غم' gives the cause ('because of/from sorrow') and 'بپرداختند' is the past plural verb 'they paid / they gave' used figuratively: 'they gave/surrendered their hearts to grief.' Word order again follows the poetic SOV pattern.
segments.s610-g149: The phrase places the indirect object/topic first: 'نخستین شهنشاه را' — 'the first shahanshah' with the object/topic marker 'را' (emphatic/definite). 'چامه‌گوی' (the bard/reciter) names the speaker; the line prepares for the reported speech that follows. Classical Persian often permits this inversion (object/topic before the noun naming the speaker).
segments.s610-g150: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said' (set phrase introducing direct speech). After it appears the vocative 'کای' (O) followed by the addressee's name/title 'خسرو' and the epithet 'ماه‌روی' ('moon-faced'). The sequence is typical: introduce the speech, then address the person vocatively.
segments.s610-g151: 'نمانی' is the negative form of ماندن ('to remain') used here with a conditional/counterfactual sense ('would not remain'); 'مگر' = 'unless / except'; 'بر فلک' = 'on/upon the sky' ('فلک' = heaven/firmament); 'ماه را' 'the moon' with 'را' as a definite/emphatic marker. Put together: 'the moon would not remain on the sky except...'.
segments.s610-g152: 'به شادی' = 'for/by the joy' (indicating cause or benefit); 'همان' = 'that same' (emphatic demonstrative); 'خسرو گاه' = 'Khosrow's time/age' with the ezāfe omitted as is common in poetry; 'را' again marks/ emphasizes 'گاه'. Together: 'for the joy of that very Khosrow's time.'
segments.s610-g57: یکی باغ = 'a certain/a garden' (indefinite noun phrase). پیش اندر آمد is a poetic verb phrase meaning 'came into view/ahead' where پیش ('before/ahead') + اندر آمد ('entered/came in') place the action in front of the perceiver; فراخ ('spacious') is a postpositive adjective qualifying the garden. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: indefinite + noun + locative/verb phrase + adjective.
segments.s610-g58: برآورده ... کاخ = 'a palace rising up' where برآورده is a verbal/adjectival participle meaning 'raised/erected/arisen' and links to the head noun کاخ ('palace') at the end; از گوشه باغ marks the source/location ('from a corner of the garden'). Note the participle precedes the noun in this line, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s610-g59: بشد is an archaic/past verb here ('it happened/there came/they came'), followed by the subject تازیان ('the Arabs'). با تنی چند is an accompanying phrase 'with a few men' where تنی چند is an idiomatic measure meaning 'a certain/small number (of bodies/men)'. شاه at the end is the noun in relation to which the action is understood (they came upon/near the king); poetic syntax allows this topicalizing placement.
segments.s610-g60: همی بود = past continuous/iterative 'was (then/at that time)'; لشکر is the subject/topic 'the army'. به نخچیرگاه marks the location 'at/to the hunting-ground' (به here introducing the locative phrase). The overall sense: 'the army was (located) at the hunting-ground.'
segments.s610-g61: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بهرام گور is the proper name with the epithet گور (here the onager epithet of Bahram). اندر آمد به باغ is a compound meaning 'entered/ came into the garden' (اندر آمد = 'came in/entered', followed by the destination به باغ).
segments.s610-g62: یکی جای دید = 'he saw a certain place' (object before verb is normal in Persian poetic syntax). از برش is an older/poetic locution meaning 'from it/its side' where برش is historically analyzable as بر + -ش (on/at it) — an archaic pronominal form; تند راغ = 'swift/fierce longing' (تند adjective modifying راغ 'longing, desire'). Together: 'he saw a place, and from it a sudden/strong desire seized him.'
segments.s610-g63: میان گلستان = 'in the middle/among the rose-garden' (میان marks 'in the midst/among'). یکی آبگیر = 'a certain pond/one pool' (indefinite noun phrase); the line places the locative phrase before the nominal to create a pictorial opening.
segments.s610-g64: بروبر نشسته = 'sitting opposite/facing' where بروبر is an adverbial 'face-to-face/opposite' modifying the participle نشسته ('seated'). یکی مرد پیر = 'an old man' (indefinite). The full phrase reads 'opposite it sat an old man.'
segments.s610-g49: Grammatically the clause places the predicative adjective/verb phrase ('زبون بود' — 'was humbled/abject') before the object phrase 'چنگال او را' (his claw) and the identifying noun 'کلنگ' (pickaxe). 'او را' is the object-marked possessive (literally 'his claw' with را marking the noun phrase). 'بود' is the past copula. The overall sense is causative/resultive: the pickaxe is what reduced/humbled his claw.
segments.s610-g50: 'شکاری' here means 'a hunter' or acting in the role of hunter; 'چو' is the simile 'like/as'; 'نخچیر' is 'prey'. The copula 'بود' is past 'was' and links the predicative phrase to 'او پلنگ' — 'he (او) [was] a leopard'. In short: as a hunter, to his prey he was a leopard.
segments.s610-g51: 'سرانجام' is an adverb 'at last/finally'. 'گشت' is past of 'گشتن' used here as 'became/turned' (i.e., 'came to be'), and 'از جهان ناپدید' follows to mean 'vanished from the world' — the adjective 'ناپدید' describes the resultant state. Word order (verb before the modifying prepositional phrase) is common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s610-g52: 'کلنگی' with the -ی suffix names 'a pickaxe' (indefinite); 'به چنگ آمدش' is idiomatic: 'came to his grasp' — note the enclitic pronoun '-ش' on 'آمد' marking 'to him'. 'بردمید' is an older/poetic past verb meaning 'carried off' or 'seized away' and is the main action of this clause. The subject is the pickaxe acting on him.
segments.s610-g53: 'بپرید' is a past-tense verb 'he leapt/sprang'. 'بر سان' is an archaic/comparative phrase meaning 'in the manner of' or 'like' (same function as 'چو/مثل'), so 'بر سان تیر از کمان' = 'like an arrow from the bow'. The simile modifies the manner of the leaping.
segments.s610-g54: 'یکی' here means 'someone' (indefinite). 'بازدار' is used as an agent ('one who restrains' or 'a restrainer') rather than an imperative. 'از پس' = 'from behind/after', 'اندر دمان' = 'at the hem/skirt' (دمان = hem), so the whole clause says that someone held him back from behind by his hem.
segments.s610-g55: 'دل شاه گشت ... تنگ' is a common Persian construction meaning 'the king's heart became tight' — idiomatically 'the king grew anxious/fearful'. 'گشت' is past 'became', and 'از پریدنش' ('from his leaping') is the cause expressed with 'از' plus the verbal noun/gerund 'پریدنش' (his jumping/leaping).
segments.s610-g56: 'همی' functions as an adverb here ('then/straightaway/just'), and 'تاخت' is past 'galloped/rushed'. 'از پس' again means 'after' (pursuing), and 'به آواز زنگ' adds the circumstance 'at the sound/ringing of the bell' (آواز = sound/call, زنگ = bell/ringing). Together: he rushed after at the bell's ringing.
segments.s610-g217: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when' introducing a clause. هر سه مه is a poetic plural subject ('each/all three moons' = three beautiful maidens); مه here is the poetic 'moon' = beauty. اندر عماری places the action 'inside a pavilion/room' (اندر = 'in/within', عماری = 'building/room'). نشست is the past tense 'sat/settled' and completes the temporal clause (verb after location in this poetic inversion).
segments.s610-g218: ز رومی همان خادم is a noun phrase 'that same servant from Rûm/Byzantium' (ز = 'from', رومی = 'Roman/Byzantine', همان = 'that same', خادم = 'servant'). آورد is the past tense 'brought'. شست here is elliptical/poetic: it indicates washing (i.e. brought water or a basin for washing); the object (water/basin) is implied rather than stated explicitly.
segments.s610-g219: به مشکوی زرین شدند is a stative phrase 'became/turned into musk‑scented and golden' with به marking the resultant state (an adverbial/qualifying preposition). شدند is the plural past 'became' and the logical subject این سه ماه ('these three moons') follows the verb in the poem's inverted word order.
segments.s610-g220: همی بود تا ... گشت شاه is a temporal/result clause: همی بود gives a continuing or habitual sense ('it continued/was so'), تا = 'until' introduces the subordinate clause. مست‌تر گشت شاه = 'the king became more intoxicated' where مست‌تر is the comparative adjective 'more drunk' and گشت is the verb 'became'; note the subject شاه appears after the verb (poetic inversion).
segments.s610-g221: بدو گفت برزین literally 'to him said Berzin': بدو = 'to him', گفت here stands for 'said' (the lemma list gives 'conversation' but function is past speech verb), and برزین is the speaker's name. که‌ای introduces the direct address (که acting as a quotative/connector here and ای as the vocative particle) before شهریار 'king'.
segments.s610-g222: جهاندار و دانا و نیزه‌گزار is a string of epithets in the vocative: 'sovereign (conqueror), and wise, and spear‑bearer.' The coordinating و simply links the honorifics; each adjective/noun stands as a title applied to the addressee.
segments.s610-g223: یکی بنده‌ام is a humble self-reference: یکی = 'a certain/one' softening the claim, بنده‌ام = 'I am a servant' (humble). تا here is the purposive 'so that / in order that', and زیم 'from me' marks source/agency; شاه را is the definite object 'the king' (را is the object marker) and is held for the verb in the next segment.
segments.s610-g224: نیایش کنم is the verb of purpose: نیایش = 'prayer/homage', کنم is the present-subjunctive/intentional form 'that I may do' (completing the purpose started by تا). خاک درگاه را = 'the dust/earth of the court' (خاک = 'dust/earth', درگاه = 'royal court/threshold'), marked by را as definite object; together the phrase means a conventional act of humble reverence 'to kiss the dust of the court.'
segments.s610-g225: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (not the numeral one) placed before the noun; بنده تازانه is the noun 'servant' with تازانه as an attributive adverb/adjective 'brisk/ fresh', and شاه را is the definite direct object marked by را. The object (شاه را) appears before the verb as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s610-g226: ببرد and بیاراست are third-person past narrative verbs (classical/Persian epic style) meaning 'took/led' and 'adorned' respectively; و links the two actions. درگاه را is the definite object of بیاراست ('the court'), again marked by را.
segments.s610-g227: سپه را uses را to topicalize/mark 'the host/army'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' linking سپه to سالار گردنکشان ('the commanders/lords who are neck‑raisers', i.e. rebellious chiefs). The modifier گردنکشان follows سالار as a descriptive compound.
segments.s610-g228: جز از = 'except (for)'; نبودی is the past negative 'there was not/there remained not', and نشان ('sign/trace') is its semantic subject. The phrase means literally 'there was no trace except from the whip' — i.e. only the whip remained as a sign.
segments.s610-g229: چو is the conjunction 'when'; دیدی here is past narrative 'he/one saw' (classical usage often uses this form with an indefinite subject); کسی is 'someone' and شاخ شیب دراز is a noun phrase 'a long slanting horn/branch' where the modifiers (شیب دراز) follow the head.
segments.s610-g230: دوان is a verbal adverb/participle 'running/rushed'; پیش رفتی is the past 'went forward/rushed on' (پیش here means 'forward'); و links the actions and بردی نماز literally 'he took/held prayer' — an idiom meaning 'performed prayer' or 'paid homage/ prostrated' in classical registers.
segments.s610-g231: همی بود is an iterative/inchoative past construction meaning 'kept on being / was still'; بهرام is the subject. تا introduces the limit 'until' and گشت مست is 'became drunk' (گشت = became, مست = drunk).
segments.s610-g232: چو = 'when'; خرم شد = 'became merry/joyful' (خرم is an adjective, شد is past 'became'); اندر + عماری = 'inside a pavilion/building' (اندر = in/within, عماری = dwelling/pavilion), and نشست is the past 'sat down/settled'.
segments.s610-g121: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. The name برزین is the subject; چنان ('thus/so') modifies the following verb دید ('saw') which is simple past 3rd‑person singular. برگشت is the main-clause verb 'returned/turned back' (past 3sg) and شاد is a predicative adjective describing the subject ('glad'), placed after the verb for a poetic, verbless adjectival complement.
segments.s610-g122: بیامد is past tense 'he came' (3rd sing.). به هر جای is a prepositional phrase 'to/into every place' (به + هر + جای). خمی نهاد is verb + object where نهاد is the past of نهادن 'put/placed'; خمی is a noun whose exact meaning is idiomatic/archaic here (see senses). Grammatically this clause continues the narrative action after برگشت.
segments.s610-g123: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. شد + مست forms a copular construction 'became drunk/intoxicated' (شد = become, مست = intoxicated/figuratively enamored). برزین is the subject. The following ب + دان + دختران functions together as a prepositional/target phrase 'by/with those maidens' (the orthography is split here but together mean 'with/at those maidens'), marking what caused the state.
segments.s610-g124: چنین گفت is a phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' — here past tense 3rd sing.). کای is a vocative 'O' addressing the following addressees. پرخرد is an adjective 'very wise', and مهتران is the plural address 'elders/chiefs' (poetic/archaic plural). Together they form the direct address: 'O very‑wise elders'.
segments.s610-g125: بدین باغ is a locative phrase 'to/into this garden' (ب + دین here functioning as the deictic 'this' in composition; باغ = 'garden'). بهرامشاه is the subject (proper name/title), and آمدست is a compound/poetic past 'has come' (3rd person). The entire segment states arrival to the garden.
segments.s610-g126: نه is the negation 'not'. گردنکشی means 'arrogant defiance/proud bearing'. با سپاه آمدست is an adjunct 'he came with an army' (با = 'with', سپاه = 'army', آمدست = 'has come'). Grammatically the clause denies that his coming was a defiant, army‑led invasion.
segments.s610-g127: هلا is an interjection ('hey' / 'come on'). چامه پیش آور‌ای is an imperative with vocative: چامه 'poem', پیش 'forward/in the presence (of the court)', آور‌ای is the imperative stem آور 'bring' plus vocative suffix -ای (addressing the addressee). چامه‌گوی is a vocative noun 'bard/poet' (lit. 'poem‑speaker'). The clause is a direct command to the bard to present a poem.
segments.s610-g128: تو is the vocative/2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. چنگ آور‌ای is again an imperative: چنگ 'harp', آور 'bring' with the vocative suffix -ای. دختر ماه‌روی is the vocative describing the addressee 'maiden of moon‑face' (i.e. beautifully faced). Grammatically this is a second command addressed to the maiden to bring the harp.
segments.s610-g169: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' here, introducing a temporal clause. آن چامه is a demonstrative + noun 'that poem/ode' (آن before the noun is normal word order in poetry). بشنید is the past tense verb 'he heard'; the full clause places the verb before the subject (بهرام گور) for poetic word order: 'When that poem he heard, Bahram Gūr...'. بهرام گور is the subject with گور used as an epithet ('Gūr' = the Onager), a name attached after the proper name.
segments.s610-g170: بخورد is the past-tense of خوردن used here in the sense 'drank/consumed' (poetically one 'drinks' the cup). آن گران‌سنگ جام بلور is the definite object: آن ('that') + modifier گران‌سنگ (here 'precious/heavy') + head جام ('cup') + qualifier بلور ('crystal'). The object marker را is omitted in this phrase (poetic compression), so the verb directly takes the noun phrase.
segments.s610-g171: بدو گفت is a verb phrase meaning 'he said to him' (بدُو = 'to him/unto him' is the prepositional clitic + object, followed by گفت 'said'); word order places the indirect object before the verb in poetry. شاه‌ای is a vocative 'O king' (شاه with the vocative -ای), but here functions as 'the king' addressing/announcing; سرافراز مرد is a compound epithet 'exalted/honored man' in apposition to the addressee.
segments.s610-g172: چشیده is a perfect participle meaning 'having tasted' or more generally 'experienced' and introduces a descriptive relative idea about the addressee. ز گیتی is the preposition z ('of/from') + گیتی ('the world'); بسی here means 'much' or 'many' in poetic diction; گرم و سرد are coordinate adjectives 'hot and cold' (literal heats and colds, i.e. the trials of life). The whole segment describes someone who has undergone many worldly ups and downs.
segments.s610-g173: نیابی is the negative present/future sense of یابیدن ('you will not find' / 'you do not find'); the initial negative ن is fused to the verb. تو is the emphatic 2nd-person pronoun. داماد بهتر ز من is the noun phrase 'a son‑in‑law better than me' (بهتر ز = 'better than'). Word order places the negative verb before the subject for emphasis in poetry.
segments.s610-g174: گو is the imperative verb 'tell/say' (a simple archaic imperative form). شهریاران is the plural 'kings/lords' (direct object or indirect addressees). سر انجمن is a compound meaning 'those who are the heads/presiders of the assembly' (سر = 'chief, head' + انجمن = 'assembly/council'); together it specifies whom to tell.
segments.s610-g175: بمن ده is the imperative phrase 'give to me' (بمن = 'to me', ده = imperative of دادن 'give'). تو این هر سه دخترت را is the object: تو ('you') + این ('these/this') + هر سه ('all three') + دخترت ('your daughters') + را (the definite object marker). The order places the indirect object and imperative before the full object phrase in poetic style.
segments.s610-g176: به کیوان برافرازم is a result clause 'I will exalt (them/him) to Kiwan': به + noun indicates the target/rank (کیوان = the planet Saturn / astrological rank) and برافرازم is the 1st‑person singular present/futureive 'I will raise/exalt'. اخترت را is the object 'your chosen one' or 'your star' (اخترت = 'your chosen/your star') with را marking it as definite object; the clause promises to raise the chosen daughter to a high celestial rank.
segments.s610-g161: دلت is the noun دل with the 2nd-person enclitic -ت, meaning 'your heart'. همچو دریا is a simile: همچو = 'like', دریا = 'the sea'. The clause then adds a second simile with و (and) رایت چو ابر: رایت = 'your banner/standard' here (not the Arabic verb), چو = 'like', ابر = 'a cloud'. Word order places the possessed noun+enclitic before its simile phrase.
segments.s610-g162: شکارت is شکار with the 2nd-person suffix -ت, here meaning 'your quarry/your hunt' (the object of hunting). نبینم is first-person present negative ('I do not see' / 'I see not'); همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'ever/always' or 'indeed'; جز = 'except'; هژبر is a proper name (Hežbar). Together: 'I see nothing of your quarry except Hežbar.'
segments.s610-g163: همی is an adverb of continuity/emphasis ('always/indeed'). مو here means 'a hair' (a single strand). شکافی is a present-tense verbal form meaning 'you split/cleave' (action performed by the addressee). به introduces the instrument/means ('with/by'), and پیکان تیر together indicate 'the (point/of the) arrow'—a poetic doublet meaning the arrow's tip.
segments.s610-g164: همی again provides continuity/emphasis. آب is 'water'. گردد (گردیدن) is the impersonal/third-person singular present meaning 'becomes' or 'turns into'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'by virtue of'. داد تو literalizes the agent/source as 'your giving/what you give' (here taken as bounty/gift), and شیر in this context is 'milk' (a nourishing substance), so the clause says 'by your giving even water becomes milk.'
segments.s610-g165: سپاهی is a collective noun 'army'. که is the relative pronoun 'that/which'. بیند is the third-person singular present 'sees' (the verb governing the clause). کمند is 'lasso/snare' (the object seen). ترا here is composed of the possessive enclitic -ت ('your') attached to کمند plus the object marker را, so the phrase reads 'your lasso' (as the object of seeing).
segments.s610-g166: همان is a demonstrative intensifier 'the same' / 'that very'. بازوی is 'arm' (here meaning both physical arm and metaphorical strength). زورمند is an adjective 'mighty/powerful' modifying بازوی. ترا again is the 2nd-person suffix + object marker, marking possession/emphasis: 'your mighty arm' (with emphatic/definitive را). The phrase stresses that seeing the lasso is tantamount to seeing the hero's mighty arm.
segments.s610-g167: به is the preposition 'to/for'. درد دل is a compound meaning 'the pain/sorrow of the heart' (درد qualified by دل). و مغز جنگاوران adds 'and the marrow/core of warriors' (مغز here = 'core, essence'), with جنگاوران the plural 'warriors'. The whole phrase is a prepositional adjunct indicating who feels the pain or what is affected.
segments.s610-g168: وگر is an adversative/conditional particle 'if/though/even if'. چند here has a sense of extent 'however many/how much' (i.e., whatever the number). باشد is the third-person subjunctive/present of 'to be' ('may be' / 'is'), governing the following noun سپاهی 'army'. گران is an adjective here meaning 'great/heavy/mighty' (often 'formidable'). Together: 'even if the army be very great.'
segments.s610-g209: This is a nominal apposition: مهین (a personal name) is followed by دخترم ('my daughter' with the possessive suffix -م) and then نام + ماه‌آفرید states her given name. Persian often omits the copula 'is' in such name-apposition phrases, so the whole phrase reads 'Mahin, my daughter, (is) named Māh‑āfrīd.'
segments.s610-g210: A short list of names/titles: فرانک دوم is 'Frank the Second' (ordinal دوم follows the name), و is the coordinating 'and', then سیوم شنبلید is another name with the ordinal سیوم ('the thirtieth') placed before the proper name شنبلید — a slightly marked/poetic word order but clear as 'Shenbleed the thirtieth.'
segments.s610-g211: پسندیدشان is the past verb 'liked/pleased' with the third‑person plural object clitic -شان 'them' attached; Persian commonly places the verb before the subject here, so شاه ('the king') follows the verb. چون دیدشان is a subordinate temporal clause 'when he saw them' (چون used as 'when/since'), with دیدشان 'he saw them' again carrying the object clitic.
segments.s610-g212: ز here means 'from/among' and introduces the group 'بانو زنان' ('lady‑women' = noble ladies); نیز 'also/likewise' modifies the following verb, and بگزیدشان is the past tense 'he chose them' with the same object clitic -شان. The subject (the king) is omitted here and understood from the previous clause.
segments.s610-g213: به برزین ('to Barzin') uses به as the preposition 'to/for' marking the indirect object. چنین گفت 'thus he said' is a formulaic phrase; کاین introduces reported content ('that these...') and هر سه ماه means 'each/ all three ماه' where ماه is used poetically for 'beautiful one' (i.e. a beauty). Note the absence of a copula — this is a report clause.
segments.s610-g214: پسندید here expresses 'pleased' (in Persian psychological constructions the thing that pleases can be the grammatical subject while the person pleased appears after the verb); چون دید is a temporal clause 'when he saw (them)' and بهرامشاه ('King Bahram') is the experiencer placed after پسندید. The verb forms are past tense.
segments.s610-g215: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a purpose/result clause here meaning 'so that' or 'that' and governs a subjunctive: مهد زرین چهار = 'four golden cradles.' The word order (noun + adjective + numeral) is slightly inverted for poetic rhythm; in prose one would more commonly expect numerals before the noun or adjective placement to differ.
segments.s610-g216: بیارد is a third‑person subjunctive/imperative form 'let him bring / that (someone) bring' used under the control of بفرمود تا. ز لشکر means 'from the army/host,' and یکی نامدار is 'a certain/famed one' (یکی acting as 'a certain'). Together: 'that a famed man be brought from the host.'
segments.s610-g137: چنین گفت برزین is a compact clause meaning “thus said Barzīn”: چنین (thus) precedes the verb گفت (said), with the proper name برزین following the verb — a common poetic inversion. که‌ای شهریار is a vocative/continuation marker introducing the addressee: که‌ای (that/O) + شهریار (sovereign), addressing the king before the following clause.
segments.s610-g138: مبیناد is an archaic/poetic third‑person form meaning “be seen/appear” or used optatively (“may ... be seen”); it governs the following circumstantial phrase بی‌تو (“without you”) and the indefinite کسی (“anyone/no one”). روزگار is the noun 'life/time/world' that is being seen/experienced. The overall phrase expresses a wish that no one’s life be (or be felt) without you.
segments.s610-g139: چنان دان که is an instruction-clause: چنان (“so/thus”) + دان (imperative/verb 'consider' or 'know') + که (that) introduces the content to be accepted. این دلبران منند is the content: این (these) + دلبران (beloved ones) + منند (a poetic copular/possessive form meaning 'are mine' or 'belong to me' in this context), so the speaker claims these beloveds as his.
segments.s610-g140: پسندیده is a past‑participle/adjective meaning 'pleasing' or 'chosen/approved' and here predicates the women implicitly (i.e. '(they are) chosen/pleasing'). و is the conjunction 'and', then دختران منند repeats the copular/possessive structure: 'the maidens are mine'. The verb/copy is elided and supplied from the previous clause.
segments.s610-g141: یکی ... و یکی is a distributive structure meaning 'one ... and another (one) ...' where یکی functions as 'one (of them)'. چامه‌گوی is a compound noun 'poet/bard/reciter' and چنگ‌زن is 'harp‑player' or 'lute player'. The copula 'is' is omitted (typical in Persian lists).
segments.s610-g142: سیم پای is an adjectival phrase 'silver-foot(ed)' (سیم 'silver' modifying پای 'foot'). کوبد is a poetic/present form 'strikes' or 'beats', and شکن بر شکن is a repeated nominal phrase 'breaker upon breaker' with بر meaning 'upon/on', so the clause says the silver‑footed one beats/strikes, breaker upon breaker (a vivid poetic image).
segments.s610-g143: چهارم is the ordinal 'the fourth' (implied subject). به کردار 'in the manner/character of' (به = 'in/with', کردار = 'demeanor/behavior'), خرم بهار = 'joyful/verdant spring' (خرم modifies بهار). Together: 'the fourth (is) in the guise of a joyful spring.'
segments.s610-g144: بدین سان is a fixed phrase 'in this way / thus' (here written with the parts ب + دین + سان); که introduces a relative/content clause. بیند همی شهریار places the verb before the subject in poetic order: بیند (he sees) + همی (continuous/ever) + شهریار (the king) — 'the king ever sees (her)'.
segments.s610-g201: ز is the poetic preposition ('because of/at') marking the target of the laugh; برزین is the proper name. بخندید is a past (narrative) form 'laughed' with the verbal prefix ب‑, and the subject (بهرام) follows the verb (V S order is common in epic Persian). و گفت follows as a connective + past verb 'and (he) said', introducing reported speech.
segments.s610-g202: The first که introduces reported content ('that'); چیزی = 'something'. The second که is a relative 'which/that' introducing the subordinate clause 'which you have' (داری تو — verb before pronoun is a common poetic order). اندر is the locative preposition 'in/within', and نهفت is a past participle/adjectival predicate 'hidden' with the copula omitted (classical Persian often omits است).
segments.s610-g203: بمان is the 2nd-person imperative 'remain/stay'. تا introduces a subordinate temporal/purpose clause ('until/so that'), and بباشد is a 3rd-person subjunctive/optative 'may he be' or 'let him be'. هم‌انجا 'there as well' and به جای 'in place of / in your stead' complete the clause: 'stay until (someone) is there in your stead.'
segments.s610-g204: تو is the vocative/second‑person pronoun 'you'. با جام is an instrumental prepositional phrase 'with a cup'. می‌سوی is present/inchoative 'you turn/go toward', taking a directional/complement. رامش‌گرای is a compound: رامش 'solace/tranquility' plus گرای functioning like a formative meaning 'seeking/‑oriented', so the phrase means 'go seek consolation' or 'turn toward comfort.'
segments.s610-g205: بدو is a clitic/prepositional phrase 'to him' (i.e., 'said to him'). پیر is 'the old man/elder' (agent), and گفت is the past verb 'said'. The reported clause این سه دختر چو ماه is a simile: این سه دختر 'these three daughters' + چو 'like' + ماه 'the moon' (i.e. very beautiful). Word order here is object/phrase after the reporting verb, typical in narrative.
segments.s610-g206: به راه is a prepositional phrase 'for/on the way of' or 'fit for/the lineage of' that qualifies the daughters' intended match. کیومرث و هوشنگ شاه are the two proper names joined by و; شاه is the title 'king' attached to هوشنگ. The phrase indicates to whom (or to whose line) the daughters are suitable.
segments.s610-g207: ترا (written here as ت + را) is the object pronoun 'to you' (ت + definite object marker را = 'you'). دادم is the 1st‑person past 'I gave'. و connects to the prayer/optative clause خاک پای 'dust of (one's) foot' — an idiom meaning humble devotion — followed by تواند, a 3rd‑person subjunctive/optative 'may he be' (classical use of تواند to express a wish or potential).
segments.s610-g208: همه هر سه combines for emphasis ('all three'); زنده is a predicative adjective 'alive'. برای here means 'for/for the sake of (you)', and تواند again is the optative/subjunctive 'may they be / may it be possible'. Together the clause expresses a wish: 'may all three be alive for you.'
segments.s610-g185: همان این is an emphatic demonstrative pair — both words mark and intensify 'these/that very' — followed by the numeral phrase 'سه دختر' (three daughters). 'پرستنده‌اند' is the present participle used predicatively with the 3rd-person plural ending -اند, meaning 'they are worshippers' or 'they worship.' Word order is topical (demonstrative + noun) then predicate.
segments.s610-g186: 'به پیش تو' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in your presence / before you' (به + پیش + pronoun). 'بر پای' literally 'on (their) feet' marks posture/position, and 'چون' introduces a simile ('like/as'). The clause ends with the predicate 'بنده‌اند' (3rd-person plural), 'they are servants/they act like servants.' Note the verb-final structure typical of Persian.
segments.s610-g187: The object 'پرستندگان' (the worshippers) is marked by the definite object marker 'را' before the past-tense verb 'پسندید' (he liked/approved). The subject 'شاه' follows the verb, a common Persian word order used for emphasis or narrative style (object + verb + subject).
segments.s610-g188: ب + دان + سان form the archaic/poetic phrase 'بدان سان' meaning 'in that manner / so' (the words are split for metrical reasons). 'که' introduces the subordinate clause 'that...'. 'از دور' is a prepositional phrase 'from afar', and 'دیدش' is the past verb 'saw / beheld' with the object clitic -ش attached (referring back to the worshippers/daughters). 'سه ماه' is a poetic simile: 'three moons'—'ماه' here is figurative for a beautiful person/face.
segments.s610-g189: 'به بالای' is a locative phrase 'on top of / atop.'
